questions.question:
Following table gives data on tourists from different countries visiting India in the year 2011:

Country       | Number of tourists
USA           | 2000
England       | 3000
Germany       | 800
Italy         | 600
Japan         | 2400
Australia     | 2200
France        | 1000

Which two countries contributed to exactly one third of the total number of tourists who visited India in 2011?

A. USA and Japan
B. USA and Australia
C. England and France
D. Japan and Australia
questions.to_find: pair of countries whose combined tourist count equals exactly one third of total tourists visiting India in 2011

understand.plain: The table lists the number of tourists from seven countries who visited India in 2011. We need to find the pair (from the given options) whose combined count equals 1/3 of the overall total. First compute the grand total, then evaluate each option pair.

keywords.explain: Exactly 1/3 of the grand total; if the grand total is T, we seek a pair summing to T/3.
Sum of tourists from all countries in the table.
The combined tourist count of exactly two countries compared against T/3.

understand.visual_alt: Table of tourist counts by country with England and France highlighted as the pair summing to 4000, which is exactly one third of the total 12000.

given.aim: Find which pair of countries together account for exactly 1/3 of all tourists visiting India in 2011

terms.meaning: USA=2000, England=3000, Germany=800, Italy=600, Japan=2400, Australia=2200, France=1000
terms.example: England alone has 3000 tourists, France alone has 1000
terms.connects: Their sum gives the grand total from which 1/3 is calculated

terms.meaning: Sum of all tourists across all seven countries
terms.connects: Dividing T by 3 gives the target value 4000 for the qualifying pair

terms.meaning: The exact combined count that the qualifying pair must equal
terms.connects: We match each option pair's sum against 4000 to identify the answer

given.plan: Add all tourist counts to find grand total T; compute T/3; then check each option pair (A=USA+Japan, B=USA+Australia, C=England+France, D=Japan+Australia) to find which sums to T/3.

steps.title: Compute grand total of tourists
steps.formula_raw: T = sum of all country counts

steps.title: Find one-third target
steps.note: We need a pair of countries whose tourists sum to exactly 4000.

steps.title: Evaluate each option pair
steps.apply:
A: USA+Japan = 2000+2400 = 4400 (not 4000)
B: USA+Australia = 2000+2200 = 4200 (not 4000)
C: England+France = 3000+1000 = 4000 (exactly 1/3)
D: Japan+Australia = 2400+2200 = 4600 (not 4000)
steps.note: Only option C yields exactly 4000, confirming England and France together account for exactly one third of all tourists.

solution.result: England and France together had 3000+1000 = 4000 tourists, which is exactly 12000/3 = 1/3 of the total. Answer: C

questions.formula_note: Only the basic sum rule is needed; no advanced formula is required for this tabular enumeration problem.

questions.question:
Following table provides figures (in rupees) on annual expenditure of a firm for two years 2010 and 2011:

Category             | 2010  | 2011
Raw material         | 520   | 650
Power & fuel         | 70    | 96.50
Salary & wages       | 1000  | 1200
Plant & machinery    | 6000  | 6500
Advertising          | 500   | 625
Research & Development | 2000 | 2500

In 2011, which of the two categories have registered increase by same percentage?

A. Raw material and Salary & wages
B. Salary & wages and Advertising
C. Power & fuel and Advertising
D. Raw material and Research & Development
questions.answer: D. Raw material and Research & Development
questions.to_find: pair of expenditure categories that show the same percentage increase from 2010 to 2011

understand.plain: The table gives expenditure (in rupees) under six categories for the years 2010 and 2011. We must compute the percentage increase for each category and identify the pair that recorded the same growth rate. Percentage increase = (new - old) / old * 100.

keywords.explain: Ratio of the absolute increase to the base year value, expressed as a percentage.
The reference year (2010) against which growth is measured.
keywords.example: For Raw material, base = 520; for R&D, base = 2000.

keywords.explain: Two categories are said to have the same increase when their growth rates (in %) are equal, even if absolute changes differ.
keywords.example: Raw material grows by 25% and R&D also grows by 25%, despite different absolute amounts.

understand.visual_alt: Table comparing 2010 and 2011 expenditures with percentage increase computed for each category.

given.aim: Identify which pair among the four options has equal percentage increase from 2010 to 2011

terms.meaning: 2010 and 2011 figures (in rupees) for six categories
terms.connects: These feed the percentage increase formula for each category
Applying this to all categories lets us find matching rates

terms.meaning: A: Raw+Salary, B: Salary+Advertising, C: Power+Advertising, D: Raw+R&D
terms.example: Option D checks if Raw material % = R&D %
terms.connects: We eliminate options by computing rates and comparing within each pair

given.plan: Compute the percentage increase for all six categories: Raw material, Power & fuel, Salary & wages, Plant & machinery, Advertising, and R&D. Then compare pairs in each option to find the matching rate.

steps.title: Compute percentage increase for each category
steps.note: Raw material, Advertising, and R&D all show exactly 25% increase.

steps.title: Check each option pair for equal percentage
steps.formula_raw: Pair matches if % increase of category_1 = % increase of category_2
steps.apply:
A: Raw (25%) vs Salary (20%) => NOT equal
B: Salary (20%) vs Advertising (25%) => NOT equal
C: Power (37.86%) vs Advertising (25%) => NOT equal
D: Raw (25%) vs R&D (25%) => EQUAL
steps.note: Option D is the only pair where both categories grew by the same 25% rate.

solution.result: Raw material and Research & Development both registered a 25% increase from 2010 to 2011. Answer: D

questions.formula_note: The percentage increase formula (new - old) / old * 100 is a standard arithmetic calculation not separately catalogued in the registry.
